Epistemology: A friend says, "I know it is true because everyone is saying it." What would you check first?
Two teachers give different answers about the same event. How would you decide which answer is stronger?
You read a confident post online with no source attached. What would make it worth trusting?
A family story has been repeated for years. What evidence would help you know whether it is accurate?
Someone changes their mind after seeing new facts. When is that wisdom instead of weakness?
A video looks real but could be edited. What clues would help you decide?
A doctor, a friend, and an influencer all give advice. How would you rank their credibility?
You believe something strongly because it feels obvious. What test could challenge that belief?
A rumor turns out to be partly true. What would you still need to know before accepting the full story?
What is one belief you would be willing to update if better evidence showed up?
Philosophy of History: After Rome falls, one person blames weak leaders and another blames economic pressure. Which explanation is stronger, and why?
A history textbook calls someone a hero, while another calls the same person dangerous. What facts would you compare?
During the American Revolution, how would a loyalist and a patriot tell the same event differently?
When studying an empire, what matters more: the ruler's personality, the economy, or the institutions?
A country wins a war and writes the official story. What evidence might be missing?
How would you judge a historical figure who did something normal then but harmful by today's standards?
What lesson from the fall of a powerful civilization could apply to a modern company or country?
If a leader gets credit for a movement, how would you check whether conditions made the movement possible?
How can maps, monuments, and holidays shape what people remember about history?
Pick a historical event you know. What is one question that would make the explanation less one-sided?
Logic & Debate: Someone says, "My team won after I wore this shirt, so the shirt is lucky." What is wrong with the reasoning?
In a classroom debate, one person attacks the speaker instead of the argument. How would you bring it back to the claim?
A friend says, "If you disagree with me, you must support the opposite." What third option might exist?
Someone gives one dramatic example and treats it like proof. What question should you ask?
You are arguing for a school policy change. What claim, evidence, and counterargument would you prepare?
A debate opponent makes one good point. How can you admit that without giving up your whole position?
Someone uses a big statistic without explaining where it came from. What would you ask next?
A person says, "This happened before, so it will happen again." What would make that comparison fair or unfair?
How would you explain the strongest argument against your own view in a way your opponent would respect?
What sentence in your argument needs the clearest definition before anyone can judge it?
Strategic Thinking: In chess, a player sacrifices a piece to win later. What is a real-life version of that kind of trade?
A student can win an argument today or protect a friendship long term. What should they consider?
A business lowers prices to beat a competitor. What second problem might that create later?
A coach benches a star player for breaking a rule. Why might that help the whole team later?
You can act quickly with little information or wait for better information. What would decide the choice?
A leader stays quiet during a conflict instead of responding immediately. When could that be strategic?
What is one move that looks slow now but could create a stronger position later?
If you had only one chance to ask a question before making a decision, what would you ask?
What choice in your life is reversible, and what choice would be hard to undo?
What would a patient opponent notice about your plan that you might miss?
Ethics & Values: In Les Miserables, Jean Valjean steals bread to survive. How should need, law, and harm be weighed?
A friend tells you a secret that could hurt someone else. When should loyalty give way to responsibility?
A school rule is fair for most students but unfair for one unusual case. Should the rule bend?
A company can make more money by hiding a small risk. What value should guide the decision?
Someone apologizes only after getting caught. How should intention and outcome be judged?
A leader must choose between honesty and keeping people calm. What should come first?
A teammate cheats to help your group win. What do you owe the team, the rules, and yourself?
A city can help many people but will harm a small neighborhood. What questions should be asked before deciding?
When is forgiveness wise, and when does it allow the same harm to continue?
What value would you still defend if it cost you popularity?
Literature & Wisdom: In The Odyssey, Odysseus is clever but often proud. When does cleverness become a weakness?
In Macbeth, ambition turns destructive. What warning sign shows ambition is becoming dangerous?
In Animal Farm, language is used to control people. What modern example feels similar?
In Romeo and Juliet, emotion moves faster than judgment. What decision needed more time?
In To Kill a Mockingbird, courage is not just physical. What does moral courage look like today?
In The Great Gatsby, a dream becomes an illusion. How can a person tell hope from self-deception?
In a story where a character betrays a friend, what motive would you look for besides simple evil?
What character from a book or movie learns too late? What should they have noticed earlier?
What story shows a person confusing pride with strength?
Which fictional mistake have you seen people repeat in real life?
Worldview & Cultures: In one culture, direct honesty is respected; in another, indirect speech protects harmony. How should people avoid misunderstanding each other?
A tradition seems strange from the outside. What should you learn before judging it?
Two families define success differently: one values independence, another values duty. How can both make sense?
A person moves to a new country and misreads social rules. What questions should they ask first?
A holiday means pride to one group and pain to another. How should both views be understood?
When does respecting another worldview become avoiding honest disagreement?
What belief from your background feels obvious but might not feel obvious elsewhere?
A culture values elders' advice more than individual choice. What are the strengths and risks?
How can food, language, and family roles shape what people think is normal?
What question would help you understand a worldview you disagree with?
Media Literacy: A headline says, "Everyone is furious." What would you check before believing that?
A viral clip starts in the middle of an argument. What missing context could change the story?
A celebrity gives advice about health, money, or politics. What makes them credible or not credible?
A creator posts an emotional story and sells a product right after. What incentive should you notice?
A chart looks convincing but has no source. What would you ask before sharing it?
A news story uses one shocking photo. How could the image shape your reaction before you read?
A trend makes an opinion popular overnight. What social reward might be driving it?
Two outlets report the same event with different words. Which words reveal the framing?
A meme makes a serious issue funny. What does it clarify, and what does it hide?
What is one question you should ask before reposting something that makes you angry?
People & Leadership: A team member takes credit for group work. How should the leader respond without creating drama?
A friend is disrespectful in public but apologizes in private. What boundary would be fair?
A coach lets the best player break rules. What message does the team receive?
A manager avoids hard feedback to stay liked. What problem grows over time?
Two friends are arguing, and both want you to take their side. What would a fair response look like?
A leader has bad news to share. What should be said clearly, and what should be handled privately?
Someone acts confident but keeps avoiding responsibility. What signal should you watch?
A group rewards the loudest person, not the best idea. How can a leader change that?
When does kindness need a boundary to stay useful?
How do you tell the difference between loyalty and covering for someone?
Creative Thinking: A musician has only three chords. How could the limit make the song more creative?
A designer cannot use color. What other tools could make the design work?
A writer is stuck on a story ending. What would happen if the hero failed instead of won?
A business has no money for ads. What creative way could it earn attention?
A student has to explain science without slides. What object or story could make the idea clear?
A chef is missing the main ingredient. How could the constraint create a better dish?
What boring rule in a project could be turned into a strength?
How would a beginner solve a problem differently from an expert?
When should you stop brainstorming and test one idea?
What idea becomes stronger when you make it simpler?
Work & Ambition: A worker stays late every night but avoids the hardest task. Is that ambition or avoidance?
A student wants credit for a group project. Who needs to see the work, and when?
A person takes a promotion that pays more but teaches less. What should they consider?
A mentor gives advice that worked 20 years ago. How would you decide whether it still fits?
A teammate is busy all day but produces little. What system would improve the work?
A person wants success quickly and skips skill-building. What cost might appear later?
When does asking for recognition help your reputation, and when does it hurt it?
What skill would raise your ceiling more than working extra hours?
A shortcut gets fast results but creates weak habits. When is it worth it?
What should someone stop doing if they want real progress faster?
Financial Judgment: A teenager wants to buy a phone with monthly payments. What cost matters besides the sticker price?
A friend says, "This investment cannot lose." What question should you ask first?
Someone gets a raise and immediately raises their spending. What risk appears later?
A business can borrow money to grow faster. What would make the debt smart or dangerous?
You can save money now or buy something that may help you earn later. How would you compare the choices?
A sale says 40% off, but you did not need the item. What is the real cost?
A risky choice has a big upside. What downside would you need to survive?
A person assumes they will earn more next year. How could that assumption distort today's decision?
When does waiting create financial value instead of just delaying?
What evidence would make you trust a financial opportunity more?
Science & Evidence: A study says coffee makes people live longer. What else might explain the result?
A medicine works for one person you know. Why is that not enough proof for everyone?
A graph looks dramatic because the scale starts near the top. How does that change your reaction?
A class experiment works once. What would repeating it prove?
A company funds research about its own product. What should you check?
A headline says two things are linked. What would show one actually causes the other?
A sample includes only volunteers. How could that bias the result?
A weather forecast says 70% chance of rain. What does that mean in practical terms?
What would a better experiment control for?
What result would make you update your view?
Technology & AI: A student uses AI to write an essay. What learning might be lost, and what help might be useful?
A company uses an algorithm to choose who gets interviews. What could go wrong?
A smart device makes life easier but collects private data. What tradeoff should be considered?
A navigation app always picks the fastest route. What might it ignore?
A chatbot gives a confident answer that is wrong. Who should be responsible for checking it?
A school bans phones completely. What problem does that solve, and what problem might it create?
A tool saves time so people stop practicing a skill. When does convenience become dependency?
An app rewards attention, not well-being. What behavior will it train?
What should AI refuse to optimize, even if users ask for it?
What human judgment should stay involved when technology makes a recommendation?
Health & Habits: A person wants to sleep better but keeps their phone beside the bed. What cue is hurting the habit?
Someone starts a hard workout plan and quits in a week. What smaller system might last?
A student snacks when stressed, not hungry. What reward is the habit giving?
A person says, "I just need more discipline." What environment change might help more?
A habit works during calm weeks but fails during busy weeks. What should the plan account for?
Someone wants to eat healthier but shops while hungry. What decision should happen earlier?
What small friction would make a bad habit harder?
What identity story helps a healthy habit stick?
When are you most likely to break a plan, and why?
What evidence would show whether your habit plan is realistic?
Civic Thinking: A city wants more safety cameras. What privacy tradeoff should citizens debate?
A school changes a rule to help most students, but a few are hurt by it. What should leaders consider?
A tax pays for a public service. Who benefits, who pays, and who might be missed?
A new law sounds good, but people may adapt around it. What incentive could it create?
A town argues over building housing. What tradeoff exists between current residents and future residents?
A protest blocks traffic to get attention. How should rights, disruption, and public impact be weighed?
When should local choice matter more than one national rule?
A policy works in one country. What would you check before copying it elsewhere?
What evidence would show whether a public policy actually works?
How would you judge the same rule if your preferred group lost power?
Personal Identity: A person says, "That is just who I am," after hurting someone. When is identity becoming an excuse?
Someone refuses to change an opinion because their friends know them for it. What are they protecting?
A student thinks being smart means never asking for help. What belief needs updating?
A person builds their whole identity around winning. What happens when they lose?
Someone changes their style, beliefs, or goals. When is that growth instead of being fake?
A person calls themselves loyal but avoids telling the truth. What value is being confused?
What label do you protect even when it stops helping you?
Which part of your self-image needs more evidence?
What would remain true about you if one opinion changed?
Where are you confusing consistency with loyalty to an old version of yourself?
